title: Doubles
subtitle: Trinidadian fried bara with curried chickpeas and coriander chutney
excerpt: Two small fried bara (soft, turmeric-yellow dough rounds) filled with curried chickpeas and finished with coriander chutney and mango. Trinidad's defining street food, eaten for breakfast.
sourceNotes: Doubles are Trinidad's most beloved street food, descended from the Indian-Trinidadian community's adaptation of Indian flatbreads and curried chickpeas (channa). The name comes from the pairing of two bara per serving. Traditional doubles use shadow beni (culantro, Eryngium foetidum) in the coriander chutney, this recipe uses fresh coriander as a substitute. Doubles are eaten at breakfast from roadside vendors who stack the bara in plastic bags. The mango chutney is traditionally made from green mango; ripe mango or a spoonful of mango chutney from a jar is a reasonable substitute.

content.text: Doubles are sold from dawn in Trinidad, a doubles vendor with a stack of bara and a pot of channa by the side of the road is as normal a sight as a bus stop. The bara are small, soft, and slightly puffy, turmeric-yellow with cumin; the channa is a moderately spiced curried chickpea filling; the coriander chutney goes on top, and if you want mango chutney or pepper sauce, you say so. Each serving is two bara: hence the name.

The bara dough needs an hour's rest after mixing to let the yeast work and the gluten relax. Don't skip this, the dough will be much easier to stretch and the result lighter. Fry at medium heat; too hot and they puff dramatically and brown before cooking through.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{yeast-fast-action}} of fast-action yeast, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Add 180 to 200 ml of warm water, a little at a time, mixing until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms. Knead for 2 minutes until smooth. Cover the bowl and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until slightly puffed.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin. Stir for 1 minute, then add the {{chickpeas-tinned}} of drained chickpeas and 200 ml of water. Simmer for 15 minutes, mashing about a quarter of the chickpeas against the side of the pan to thicken. Season to taste.

Blend the {{coriander}} of coriander, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet (if using), and the juice of the {{lime}} lime with 3 to 4 tablespoons of water to a smooth, bright green chutney. Season with salt.

Divide the rested dough into 16 equal pieces. With oiled hands, flatten each piece into a thin round about 10 cm in diameter. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a saucepan over medium heat to 175°C. Fry the bara 2 to 3 at a time for 1 to 2 minutes per side until lightly golden and puffed. Drain on kitchen paper. To serve: place two bara side by side, spoon channa over each, and finish with coriander chutney.

items.fix: Give the dough a full hour. The yeast needs time to relax the gluten and create a little gas. Fry at 175°C, too cool and the bara absorb oil.
items.cause: Dough not rested long enough; oil not hot enough

items.fix: Lower the heat slightly. A gentle puff is correct; a dramatic balloon that collapses is oil too hot.
Mash a third of the chickpeas with a fork directly in the pot, then stir. This thickens the sauce into something that sits on the bara without running off.
Make the chutney just before serving. Add a squeeze of extra lime to brighten it. The colour fades within an hour.

content.text: Doubles are Trinidad's great street food: breakfast food, hangover food, three-in-the-morning food. They come from the Indian-Trinidadian community, the descendants of indentured workers who arrived from India in the nineteenth century and brought their cooking traditions with them. The bara draws on the fried bread tradition; the channa on curried chickpea dal. What came out of that is distinctly Trinidadian. The combination, the proportions, the shadow beni chutney all belong here and nowhere else.
